data.title: Here's what the OpenAI-AMD deal says about Nvidia
data.description:
By Britney Nguyen

OpenAI says it's buying AMD chips to get more computing power. Analysts also see benefits to diversifying away from Nvidia and likely securing favorable pricing.

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and AMD CEO Lisa Su both testified before Congress earlier this year.

OpenAI said its new plan to deploy six gigawatts' worth of Advanced Micro Devices Inc. chips will help it amass massive amounts of computing power as quickly as possible. But some analysts see another motivation.

The maker of ChatGPT is already a large customer of Nvidia Corp. (NVDA), and the companies struck a new agreement for millions of chips' worth of computing power in September. OpenAI's subsequent deal with AMD is a vote of confidence in the company's products, but also "an indication that OpenAI recognizes a need to diversify its processor supplies" as it builds out its data centers, Jack Gold, president of J.Gold Associates, wrote in a note to clients earlier this week.

Under the agreement, OpenAI will deploy six gigawatts of AMD's next-generation processors, starting with its Instinct MI450 series in the second half of next year.

The process of amassing AI chips is a balancing act for companies. There are benefits to diversification, but Nvidia is also a powerful player whose supply of products has been constrained.

OpenAI Chief Executive Sam Altman said in a post on X that the partnership with AMD "is all incremental to our work with Nvidia," and noted that the company plans to increase the number of chips it buys from Nvidia over time. In September, OpenAI said it plans to deploy 10 gigawatts' worth of Nvidia's systems, starting with its upcoming Vera Rubin platform in the second half of next year.

See more: Why Nvidia's $100 billion investment in OpenAI signals a major transformation

Nvidia's most advanced chips are often on allocation, Gold told MarketWatch, meaning that OpenAI and its other customers are limited in how many chips they can buy since demand is outstripping supply.

"If I'm a big vendor, having a sole source with allocation issues is troubling," Gold said. "It's hard to plan for that."

Meanwhile, AMD has proven that it can be a credible competitor to Nvidia, he added. And while there are disagreements over whether AMD's offerings are as good as Nvidia's, "that's not necessarily the issue if it's a viable alternative," he said, as companies are looking for more sources.

"Having a sole source in anything is problematic - that's not just true for AI, that's true for any company," Gold said.

Altman noted AMD's "leadership in high-performance chips" in a statement about the agreement. He said the partnership will enable OpenAI "to accelerate progress and bring the benefits of advanced AI to everyone faster."

Nvidia Chief Executive Jensen Huang told CNBC Investing Club's Jim Cramer on Tuesday that Nvidia and AMD "build some things that are similar in category," but that the companies are "very different."

The structures of both deals are also different, with Nvidia investing $100 billion in OpenAI in increments as its systems are rolled out, while OpenAI has taken a 10% stake in AMD, vesting with each gigawatt deployed. The vesting also depends on AMD's stock reaching certain share-price targets, and OpenAI meeting "technical and commercial" goals.

Read: AMD's stock soars to its best day in a year. Here's why OpenAI wants a stake.

In a separate interview on CNBC's "Squawk Box" Wednesday, Huang called the deal between AMD and OpenAI "unique and surprising" since AMD was "so excited about their next-generation product."

To Ray Wang, lead semiconductor analyst at The Futurum Group, the deal between OpenAI and AMD is more about the compute, and less about changing its relationship with Nvidia.

A large portion of OpenAI's chips comes from Nvidia, Wang said, adding that he doesn't expect that to change next year or in 2027, especially with the company's rack-scale and software advantages over AMD. The industry is waiting to see how AMD's first rack-scale product, Helios, will compare to Nvidia's offering, Wang said. AMD introduced Helios at its Advancing AI event in June, and it will be deployed with the first of the six gigawatts of compute that OpenAI and AMD have agreed to.

There can be multiple rationales for the OpenAI-AMD deal, according to Evan Schlossman, a principal at SuRo Capital.

"While it supports diversification away from Nvidia as a single supplier, the bigger driver right now seems to be securing enough compute capacity amid overwhelming demand," Schlossman told MarketWatch in emailed comments. "With GPU shortages and long lead times, access matters more than supplier balance."

OpenAI President Greg Brockman told CNBC's "Squawk on the Street" on Monday that OpenAI's lack of compute power is holding it back from launching more products and features that can be monetized.

See more: The AI trade increasingly hinges on OpenAI - and that's a big risk for the entire market

Another important consideration will be the price comparison between Nvidia's Rubin platform and AMD's MI450 series, Wang said. He expects the deal with AMD will give OpenAI some kind of price discount.

With more than one supplier, OpenAI has "much more pricing leverage on buying chips," Gold said in his Monday note. The leverage "could ultimately save them a great deal of money when it comes time to negotiate on pricing."

Gold told MarketWatch that customers between Nvidia and AMD are looking at buying compute from a price-performance perspective.

"The market wants more supply from different vendors, and that's what AMD is now doing," Gold said.

And while there are fears that AMD will eat into Nvidia's majority share of the market for AI chips, both Wang and Gold said the market is expanding and both companies will benefit. That also leaves room for custom chips from suppliers such as Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) and Marvell Technology Inc. (MRVL)

Looking two to three years out, however, Gold said that needs in the AI market will change as the industry shifts more toward AI inferencing, or the process of running AI models after training. Nvidia's current GPUs are optimized for AI training, he said, but inference can rely on accelerators around general-purpose CPUs from companies such as Intel Corp. (INTC) and Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM).

"That's a whole different space, and Nvidia is not as strong in that space," Gold said, adding that there are some promising startups focused on inferencing chips, too. "Training won't go away, but it will be less of the share of the pie."

-Britney Nguyen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  10-09-25 0815ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Canada's Financial Sector Needs Increased Competition, Bank of Canada Official Says
data.description:
By Paul Vieira 




  OTTAWA--The Bank of Canada's No. 2 official endorsed Thursday increased competition in the country's highly concentrated financial-services sector, saying this would help lift Canada out of its prolonged productivity slump. 



  Carolyn Rogers, the central bank's senior deputy governor, said Canadian authorities have done a stellar job in regulating banks by ensuring they have enough capital to survive shocks such as the 2008-09 financial crisis and the Covid-19 pandemic. "It would also be hard to argue, on any objective measure, that Canada's banking  system is anything other than an oligopoly," said Rogers, according to prepared remarks set for delivery before a Toronto business crowd. 



   Rogers cited data indicating the country's six largest lenders hold over 90% of the country's banking assets. The banking sector's concentration has contributed to financial stability in Canada, she said -- adding, however, that's come at the cost of greater innovation and competition. 





  Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 09, 2025 08:15 ET (12:15 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: EPIC Insurance Brokers & Consultants Expands Transportation Practice with Appointment of Kristy Neal as Senior Vice President
data.description:
EPIC Insurance Brokers & Consultants Expands Transportation Practice with Appointment of Kristy Neal as Senior Vice President

Neal to strengthen EPIC’s transportation strategy with a focus on innovation, risk management and operational efficiency for clients nationwide

EPIC Insurance Brokers & Consultants ("EPIC"), a national insurance brokerage and consulting firm providing specialized services and products across select industries, today announced the appointment of Kristy Neal as senior vice president, strategic development to its Transportation & Logistics practice. Neal will focus on integrating EPIC’s risk and insurance offerings across freight brokers and forwarders, passenger transportation and transportation risk control, while equipping its transportation team with the tools, training and resources needed to help clients strengthen their operations and improve efficiency.

Based in Vacaville, California, Neal brings over 17 years of insurance and logistics expertise to EPIC’s transportation practice. Reporting to Scott Davis, president of national specialties, she will oversee transportation initiatives spanning embedded solutions, technology partnerships, program development and client engagement. With leadership experience across insurance, transportation, logistics and supply chain – including senior roles as Head of Growth at Loadsure and Head of Enterprise Business at Cover Whale – Neal is well positioned to drive innovation and expand the value EPIC delivers to its clients.

“As someone who has built a career at the intersection of insurance, logistics and technology, I’m honored to join EPIC at such a pivotal time,” said Neal. “Transportation and logistics are the backbone of the global economy, and as the industry evolves through digital platforms and data analytics, my focus is on helping transportation clients navigate change, optimize operations, manage emerging risks and shape the future of the practice with such a talented team.”

The transportation industry is facing significant risks as global supply chains adapt to shifting trade regulations, economic uncertainty, tariffs and infrastructure limitations. With Neal’s addition, EPIC’s transportation practice is expanding to address these challenges by leveraging product specialization, consulting expertise and technology-enabled solutions to optimize both logistics and transit operations and supply chain management. Neal’s appointment strengthens EPIC’s ability to help transportation clients manage risk and position their operations for long-term success.

“EPIC’s growth strategy has always centered on building deep specializations in key industries and expanding the capabilities of our teams,” saidGregg Bundschuh, chief growth officer at EPIC. “Kristy is exactly the kind of leader who can drive that vision – helping our transportation practice innovate, address emerging risks and deliver exceptional solutions. Her leadership ensures clients benefit not only from EPIC’s existing nationwide resources, but also from the development of new tools and products designed to manage the developing risks facing the transportation industry.”

EPIC continues to deepen its expertise in industries where specialized knowledge is in greatest demand. While transportation remains a central focus, the company is also expanding its specializations in construction and design, real estate, sports and entertainment, healthcare and senior living, as well as designing tailored solutions for lawyers, accountants and dentists.

For more information on EPIC Transportation and its specialized insurance and risk management solutions for transportation and logistics businesses, please visit epicbrokers.com.

About EPIC Transportation

EPIC’s Transportation practice delivers specialized insurance and risk management solutions tailored to companies moving goods and passengers across global supply chains and transit networks. The team structures coverage to address cargo, liability, fleet operations and other business exposures, whether for freight carriers, public transit authorities or private passenger transport services. Options extend from customs bonds to cyber liability, with practical solutions such as contingent cargo coverage, warehouse liability, fleet and passenger transport insurance. These are paired with support tools like contract review software and claims management. The practice’s focus is on helping logistics and transit businesses mitigate operational risks effectively while maintaining comprehensive, cost-efficient coverage. For more information, please visit epicbrokers.com.

data.title: Jamie Dimon says there's a 30% chance of a correction. 'I'm far more worried than others.'
data.description:
By Barbara Kollmeyer

CEO of banking giant JPMorgan says some money invested in AI will be wasted

JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon says he's more worried about a stock market correction than most investors.

Dip buyers showed their teeth again on Wednesday, scooping up sold-off tech stocks in particular after Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang gave the AI story and tech names a boost by talking of a big jump in computing demand.

But simmering doubts over how much AI can keep pushing the market has been beating down on investors' heads. Weighing in on bubble talk is our call of the day, from JPMorgan Chase's CEO Jamie Dimon, who said he is "far more worried than others" about a correction in U.S. stocks."

"So if the market is pricing in 10%, I would say it's more like 30%, and I'm not saying next year because the timing of these things is almost impossible," he told the BBC in an interview that published on Thursday to coincide with an investment in the south of England. Investors could see such a downturn in 6 months or even two years, he added.

"The level of uncertainty should be higher in most people's minds than what I call normal," the head of the U.S.'s biggest bank said.

What is worrying Dimon? "You see a lot of things out there, the amount of uncertainty and I put geopolitics in that category, fiscal spending in that category, politics in that category, the re-militarization of the world in that category. All these things cause a lot of issues that we don't know how they're going to sort out," Dimon said. Rather than investing in crypto, "we should be stockpiling bullets, guns and bombs," as the world's a "far more dangerous place."

Stock valuations as high, and credit markets also indicate investors aren't seeing higher risks of default, he said.

He adds that no one was forecasting past stock crashes, but for AI there's much "hype" and money going into it right now.

While AI is real and "in total it will pay off, just like cars in total paid off, TV in total paid off, but most people involved in that didn't do well." Still, he warned that said some of the money being funneled into AI will probably be wasted.

The CEO pushed back at the idea that rising crypto and gold prices are a sign that the world has lost faith in the U.S. dollar and the country's economic leadership. "I don't think they've lost faith," but investors are "questioning a little bit more. The dollar is still the best currency in the world," he said.

Investors should consider the fact a big portion of their investments are in the U.S. dollar and perhaps that should be reduced a little.

Read: Strategist makes 'jaw-dropping' call - the dollar will rally, thanks to booming productivity

The CEO added that he also doesn't think President Donald Trump will "violate the independence of the Fed," though he's somewhat worried about inflation to the extent that the central bank may not be able to deliver the 100 basis points of cuts the market is expecting.

The markets

U.S. stock futures (ES00) (YM00) (NQ00) are holding steady, with Treasury yields BX:TMUBMUSD10Y BX:TMUBMUSD02Y edging higher. Gold (GC00), oil (CL00) and the dollar DXY are pulling back. 

   Key asset performance                                                Last       5d     1m      YTD      1y 
   S&P 500                                                              6753.72    0.63%  3.39%   14.83%   16.60% 
   Nasdaq Composite                                                     23,043.38  1.27%  5.29%   19.33%   25.98% 
   10-year Treasury                                                     4.13       4.40   10.00   -44.60   6.50 
   Gold                                                                 4056       4.51%  10.42%  53.68%   53.21% 
   Oil                                                                  62.24      2.57%  0.00%   -13.40%  -17.66% 
   Data: MarketWatch. Treasury yields change expressed in basis points

The buzz

Third-quarter reporting season is getting underway, with PepsiCo (PEP) reiterating annual guidance as third-quarter earnings edged past estimates.

Delta stock (DAL) is up after the airline reported profit and revenue that rose well above expectations.

Ferrari (RACE) shares are lower after the sports-car maker's 2030 guidance.

HSBC shares (HSBC) are falling after the bank said it was making a $14 billion bet on Hong Kong - at a hefty premium.

Fed Chairman Jerome Powell will make introductory remarks at the Community Bank Conference at 8:30 a.m., with Fed Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman at the same event. John Williams, president of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, told The New York Times that he is in favor of more interest rate cuts.

China's commerce ministry has added restrictions on rare earths exports ahead of an expected President Trump/Chinese President Xi Jinping meeting.

Best of the web

Meme-stock ETF is back from the dead. Last time, its launch coincided with the 2021 market top.

Republicans caution White House over inflicting shutdown pain on Americans.

JPMorgan reportedly paid $115 million in legal bills for Charlie Javice, a co-defendant who scammed bank out of $175 million.

The chart

BCA Research offers this chart that they say warns of job losses to come. "The U.S. labor market is now perilously close to the 'kink' in the Beveridge curve - the point where falling openings tend to correspond to rapidly rising unemployment," says Peter Berezin, chief global strategist. This Beveridge curve represents a relationship between unemployment and job opening rates and typically slopes downwards.

Top tickers

These were the top-searched tickers on MarketWatch as of 6 a.m.: 

   Ticker  Security name 
   NVDA    Nvidia 
   TSLA    Tesla 
   AMD     Advanced Micro Devices 
   GME     GameStop 
   TSM     Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing 
   PLUG    Plug Power 
   NIO     NIO 
   PLTR    Palantir Technologies 
   AMZN    Amazon.com 
   AAPL    Apple

Random reads

Lost and found: $1 million in Spanish gold and silver coins near Florida coast.

Hair no more. Missouri museum of famous hair wreaths is closing.

Rare flowers spring up in Chilean desert after freak rains.

-Barbara Kollmeyer

data.title: Lloyds Banking Group, Close Brothers Warn of Heavier Hit From U.K. Car-Loan Redress — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Elena Vardon 




  Lloyds Banking Group and Close Brothers cautioned that they would probably need to set aside more money to compensate customers as part of a probe into commissions paid on car loans, sending the shares of both U.K. banks lower. 



  "Based on our initial analysis and the characteristics of the proposed scheme, an additional provision is likely to be required which may be material," Lloyds said Thursday. In a separate statement, Close Brothers echoed that the regulator's plan is "likely to result in a material increase in its existing provision" if implemented in its current form. 



  The U.K.'s Financial Conduct Authority earlier this week shared details on a program to compensate customers for the payment of commissions that it deems were unfairly charged on car loans by dealerships. 



  The redress program, which is set to be rolled out next year, will cost the industry an estimated 11 billion pounds ($14.74 billion), the FCA said. This is based on 8.2 billion pounds in customer payouts--including interest--and 2.8 billion pounds in administrative costs, assuming that 85% of eligible customers take part. 



  Lloyds, which is the largest car-finance provider in the U.K. through its Black Horse brand, has already booked 1.15 billion pounds in provisions for this matter in previous quarters. Based on its market share, analysts calculate that the bank could face an up to 2.0 billion-pound total charge and will need to earmark more cash to cover the difference. 



  Shares fell as much as 4%, erasing gains logged on Wednesday following the initial relief surrounding the FCA's consultation and size of the potential bill, which was smaller than feared, lifted the stocks of exposed banks. 



  Close Brothers' shares also tumbled on Thursday and were down 10% in midday exchanges. The merchant bank, which has the largest relative exposure given that car finance represents a significant part of its loan book, has so far set aside a 165 million-pound provision. The group is continuing to assess the implications of the redress plan and is confident in its capital strength, it said. 



  The FCA banned discretionary commissions on car financing in 2021 and has been investigating historical agreements dating back almost two decades since early last year. The regulator estimates that 44% of car loans taken out between April 2007 and November 2024, equivalent to 14.2 million agreements, were unfair and are liable for compensation. 



  "That [Lloyds'] initial thoughts are that the number of cases in scope could be higher suggests that the 2 billion pounds may be more of a central scenario than worst case," analysts at Keefe, Bruyette & Woods wrote in a note to clients. 



  Lloyds and Close Brothers both said that uncertainties remain on the outcome of the FCA's consultation as well as the interpretation and implementation of the proposals. 





  Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 09, 2025 07:58 ET (11:58 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Delta's stock rises as a recent acceleration in travel leads to record revenue
data.description:
By Tomi Kilgore and Steve Gelsi

Air carrier's profit and revenue rose well above expectations, the full-year outlook improved and load factor snapped a seven-quarter streak of misses

Delta's stock surges, lifting those of its peers, after the air carrier beat earnings expectations as travel demand accelerated in recent weeks.

Delta Air Lines Inc.'s stock price surged Thursday after the air carrier signaled early signs of a strong holiday travel season and reported profit and revenue that rose well above expectations.

Delta (DAL) also raised its full-year earnings outlook and broke a long streak of load factor misses amid strength in premium services and corporate travel and also raised its full-year earnings outlook.

"Sales trends have accelerated across all geographies and in every advance purchase window," President Glen Hauenstein said in a statement.

And about bookings for holiday travel, Chief Executive Ed Bastian said on CNBC that while it's a little early, "it's looking good."

The stock shot up 6.2% in recent premarket trading, enough to lead the S&P 500 index's SPX gainers ahead of the open. The results also provided a boost to other airline stocks, as United Airlines Holdings Inc. shares (UAL) rallied 4.9% and American Airlines Group Inc.'s stock (AAL) climbed 4.9%.

The carrier is seeing "so much momentum in the marketplace" for travel, and that the government shutdown in the U.S. isn't expected to have a significant impact, Bastian said on CNBC.

The carrier managed through a dip in travel by European and Canadian residents flying into the U.S. by looking to boost its domestic business, with a focus on pricing, he said.

Delta said operating revenue rose 4.1% from the same period a year ago to a September-quarter record of $15.2 billion. The average analyst revenue estimate compiled by FactSet was $15.08 billion.

Passenger revenue in the U.S. increased 5%, as revenue from both premium cabins and SkyMiles members increased 9%. There was also a recent acceleration in corporate sales, which rose 8%, as surveys indicated that about 90% of companies expected travel volume to either increase or remain steady in 2026.

Net income rose 11.4% to $1.42 billion, while adjusted earnings per share were up to $1.71 from $1.50 and beat the FactSet consensus of $1.53.

Load factor slipped to 86% from 87% to match the FactSet consensus. That was the first time in six quarters that load factor didn't miss expectations, according to FactSet data.

For the full year, the company said it now expects EPS of about $6, which is above the midpoint of the previous guidance range of $5.25 to $6.25.

Delta's stock has slipped 5.6% in 2025 through Wednesday, while the U.S. Global Jets ETF JETS has eased 1.7% and the S&P 500 has gained 14.8%.

-Tomi Kilgore -Steve Gelsi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  10-09-25 0800ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Accenture Helps Organizations Advance Agentic AI with Gemini Enterprise
data.description:
Accenture Helps Organizations Advance Agentic AI with Gemini Enterprise

  • Clients such as JCOM, Radisson Hotel Group, and a U.S. health insurer are solving complex business challenges with Google AI technologies
  • Accenture and Google Cloud joint generative AI Center of Excellence is expanding with agentic capabilities
  • More than 450 agents built by Accenture are available on Google Cloud Marketplace

Accenture (NYSE: ACN) and Google Cloud announced today that their strategic alliance is driving client reinvention with Gemini Enterprise agentic AI solutions, building on the successful adoption of Google Cloud technologies for organizations across industries.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251009854228/en/

Accenture and Google Cloud are driving client reinvention with Gemini Enterprise agentic AI solutions, building on the successful adoption of Google Cloud technologies with organizations across industries.

Accenture is advancing agentic AI with support for Gemini Enterprise, a new agentic platform designed to bring the full power of Google’s AI to every employee and every workflow. With Accenture’s deep experience in cloud and AI technologies, engineering capabilities, and technical expertise on Google Cloud technology, the two companies will help drive operational efficiencies and business value using Google’s latest AI innovations.

"Working alongside Google Cloud, we're dedicated to empowering our clients' transformation using the cutting-edge AI capabilities of Gemini Enterprise,” said Scott Alfieri, Accenture Google Business Group lead. “We're creating a foundation for innovation for our clients, on a strong digital core with seamless data and AI support. This means centralizing workflows, supercharging AI agent development, and driving adoption at scale – ultimately fueling long-term success and propelling businesses into the future."

“With Gemini Enterprise, we are making it possible for every employee and workflow to benefit from the latest advancements in Google’s AI,” said Victor Morales, VP of GSI Partnerships, Google Cloud. “We’re combining our AI leadership with Accenture's strategic expertise to help clients solve their most complex challenges and achieve measurable outcomes.”

Accenture is deepening its commitment to agentic reinvention with Google Cloud through the evolution of its joint generative AI Center of Excellence (CoE) by expanding it with agentic capabilities to support clients. Powered by Google Gemini models and now with Gemini Enterprise, the CoE will provide clients with the resources they need to scale and orchestrate multi-agent systems that deliver measurable business outcomes.

The companies are delivering value and enabling clients to solve complex business challenges with AI across industries, such as:

  • JCOM collaborated with Accenture and Google Cloud on the “JAICO Project,” an AI-driven initiative designed to enhance customer experiences through deeper customer understanding. Powered by Gemini, the solution is deployed in JCOM’s customer service centers, where AI summarizes hundreds of thousands of conversation records a month, enabling operators to handle inquiries more efficiently. Moving forward, JCOM aims to further advance the use of generative AI and, by combining real and digital experiences, deliver hyper-personalized customer experiences.
  • With over 1,520 hotels in more than 100 countries, the Radisson Hotel Group strives to deliver memorable moments and speak to their customers in their own language in every interaction. The Radisson Hotel Group worked with Accenture and Google Cloud to use Vertex AI and Gemini models to personalize ads at scale in multiple languages automatically in hours instead of weeks using AI, which increased revenue and efficiency across the business. By training AI models on extensive datasets stored in BigQuery; ad teams saw productivity rise by 50% while revenue increased from AI-powered campaigns by more than 20%.
  • At a large health insurer in the United States, the integration of Google Agentspace with cloud-based enterprise collaboration and data tools has established a powerful foundation for enterprise-wide knowledge access. This connectivity enables teams to streamline workflows and better service policy holders by being able to quickly retrieve insights, documents, and communications across platforms through a single conversational interface.

To demonstrate the rapid evolution of agentic AI and its transformative potential, Accenture recently hosted a Google Agentic AI Codefest competition to build AI agents that assist organizations across 15 industries. These agents are now available through Google Cloud Marketplace and include next-gen agents, conversational agents, and AgentEngine agents. More than 450 engineered agents built by Accenture are currently available on Google Cloud Marketplace, which will be accessible directly within Gemini Enterprise so that organizations can easily combine agents into one singular view.

As a partner for Accenture’s LearnVantage digital learning services, Accenture and Google Cloud are also adding new skilling programs, enabling enterprise users to apply and gain value from Gemini Enterprise agents more quickly and effectively.

Additionally, Accenture will expand its own internal use of Google Cloud technology by integrating Gemini Enterprise to accelerate knowledge sharing and to autonomously execute individual processes or functions.

For more information on Accenture’s strategic partnership with Google Cloud to drive client reinvention with agentic AI, visit here.

About Accenture
Accenture is a leading global professional services company that helps the world’s leading businesses, governments and other organizations build their digital core, optimize their operations, accelerate revenue growth and enhance citizen services—creating tangible value at speed and scale. We are a talent- and innovation-led company with approximately 779,000 people serving clients in more than 120 countries. Technology is at the core of change today, and we are one of the world’s leaders in helping drive that change, with strong ecosystem relationships. We combine our strength in technology and leadership in cloud, data and AI with unmatched industry experience, functional expertise and global delivery capability. Our broad range of services, solutions and assets across Strategy & Consulting, Technology, Operations, Industry X and Song, together with our culture of shared success and commitment to creating 360° value, enable us to help our clients reinvent and build trusted, lasting relationships. We measure our success by the 360° value we create for our clients, each other, our shareholders, partners and communities. Visit us at accenture.com.

data.title: PepsiCo Names Walmart Executive as CFO; 3Q Revenue Rises Amid Turnaround Efforts - Update
data.description:
By Connor Hart 




  PepsiCo tapped a Walmart executive to fill its top finance role as the packaged-food company aims to turn around its slowing soda and snack sales. 



  Steve Schmitt will assume PepsiCo's CFO role effective Nov. 10, succeeding Jamie Caulfield, who plans to retire after more than three decades at the company, the company said Thursday. 



  Schmitt, 52 years old, has served as finance chief of Walmart's U.S. arm since 2021. Prior to joining the retail goliath in 2016, he held several roles of increasing leadership during a decade-long tenure at Yum! Brands. 



  In his new role, Schmitt will join a leadership team tasked with turning around a struggling business, up against an activist investor. PepsiCo in recent years has struggled to win back soda drinkers after ceding market share to rivals, while its food business--once an engine for growth--has stalled as inflation-weary shoppers cut back on snacks. 



  These struggles led Elliott Investment Management to amass a nearly $4 billion stake in PepsiCo and push for the company to sell off underperforming brands and revamp its bottling network, The Wall Street Journal previously reported. 



  Also on Thursday, PepsiCo said fiscal third-quarter revenue rose, which Chief Executive Ramon Laguarta attributed to resiliency in its international business and improved momentum in its North American beverage unit. The company has also taken action to reshape its portfolio, Laguarta said, cutting underperforming products while launching ones that lean into current trends such as health and wellness. 



  Still, profit slipped as volumes fell. The Purchase, N.Y., snacks and soda maker posted a profit of $2.6 billion, or $1.90 a share, for its quarter ended Sept. 6, compared with $2.93 billion, or $2.13 a share, in last year's comparable quarter. 



  Stripping out one-time items, earnings were $2.29 a share. Analysts polled by FactSet expected adjusted earnings of $2.26 a share. 



  Revenue edged up 2.7% to $23.94 billion, topping Wall Street's expectation of $23.84 billion. 



  In North America, volumes fell 4% across PepsiCo's food business and 3% across its beverage division. These losses were offset by volume gains across the company's Europe, Middle East and Africa beverage unit, as well as its Asia-Pacific food group. 



  Looking ahead, PepsiCo said it expects its international business to remain resilient, and for changes to its North American unit to pay off. "Right sizing our entire cost base is a priority," the company said in prepared remarks, as it aims to "mitigate incremental supply chain costs." 



  For the year, PepsiCo continues to expect a low-single-digit increase in organic revenue, as well as for earnings to be approximately flat with last year, on a constant-currency basis. 





  Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 09, 2025 07:33 ET (11:33 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Ferrari Shares Plunge as Long-Term Targets Disappoint — Update
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping 




  Ferrari shares slumped Thursday after new long-term profitability targets missed expectations. 



  Shares were 15% lower in afternoon trade. 



  The Italian luxury sports-car maker nudged its full-year financial expectations higher at an investor event in Italy while setting new financial targets for the end of the decade. 



  It said it expects net revenue to grow at 5% a year over the next five years, driven by its model lineup, including limited-edition models, and car customizations. Volumes will positively contribute, but to a lesser extent, it said. 



  Full-year adjusted earnings before interest and tax is now expected to reach at least 2.06 billion euros ($2.4 billion) from at least 2.03 billion euros previously, before hitting at least 2.75 billion euros in 2030. 



  Full-year margin guidance of at least 29% was left unchanged, with a figure of at least 30% expected in 2030. 



  The guidance implies that EBIT will grow at 6% a year through the end of the decade, well below the 10%-a-year that was implied by the company at its 2022 investor event when it gave its 2022 to 2026 forecast, RBC Capital Markets analyst Tom Narayan said in a note to clients. 



  "The 6% compound annual growth rate is likely conservative, but investors are likely to interpret a downshift in EBIT growth from prior history," he said. 



  The comments on volume growth are also weaker than expected, especially as Ferrari has increased capacity after inaugurating a new factory, and volumes are needed to keep the customer wait list from getting too long, Narayan adds. 



  "We think the 2030 guidance coming in below expectations will push shares lower today." 



  The news comes as Ferrari scaled back it electric-vehicle ambitions while guiding to a model lineup that will include a greater share of combustion engine models compared to previous forecasts. 



  Around 20% of its model lineup in 2030 will be fully-electric, marking a sharp cut to the 40% it presented in its previous plan. 



  Combustion engines are expected to make up 40% of its 2030 lineup, a greater share than the 20% it targeted at the 2022 investor day, with the share of hybrid models still expected at 40%. 



  Ferrari on Thursday revealed the first details of its first fully-electric car, the Elettrica, which is expected to launch next year. It plans to launch an average of four new cars a year between 2026 and 2030. 



  Ferrari now expects to report net revenue of at least 7.1 billion euros this year, up from 7 billion euros previously, and rising to around 9 billion euros in 2030. 



  Adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is now expected to reach at least 2.72 billion euros, from at least 2.68 billion euros previously, before hitting at least 3.6 billion euros in 2030. Full-year margin guidance of at least 38.3% was left unchanged, with a figure of at least 40% expected in 2030. 





  Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 09, 2025 07:35 ET (11:35 GMT)
